name: Bull's Blood
image_alt: Deep burgundy beet with striking dark red leaves
description: A dual-purpose beet grown as much for its stunning deep burgundy foliage as for its roots. Young leaves are among the most beautiful and flavorful salad greens available. Roots are deep red-purple with earthy, sweet flavor.
history: An heirloom dating to the 1840s in France, grown primarily as an ornamental vegetable for its striking dark foliage. The leaf color was used to indicate blood type compatibility in early folk medicine, hence the name.
seasonal_quirks: Leaf color is most intense in cool weather and full sun — heat and shade reduce the dramatic burgundy. Baby leaves harvested young are the most tender and colorful for salads.
harvest_cues: Harvest baby leaves at 2–3 inches for salads. Full roots at 2–3 inches diameter. Roots are best before they exceed 3 inches — older roots can become fibrous.
variety_notes: One of the most ornamental edibles — suitable as a border plant in flower beds. The deep color bleeds aggressively when cut — wear an apron.
preservation: Roast and freeze roots. Pickle in vinegar brine.
